Based on the search results, here's what analyst reports and recent research show about enterprise spending on AI agent security and governance tools for 2025-2026:

## Key Spending Trends

Half of executives plan to allocate $10-50 million in the coming year to secure agentic architectures, improve data lineage, and harden model governance. Nearly all — 98% — of enterprises plan to increase governance budgets in the next financial year, with the average business anticipating a 24% jump.

## Market Size & Growth

The global agentic AI market has reached between $9.14 billion and $10.86 billion in 2026, depending on the methodology used. The AI governance tools market is projected to grow from $227-340 million (2024-2025) to $4.83 billion by 2034, with a CAGR of 35-45%.

## Critical Security & Governance Gaps

Gartner forecasts that 40% of enterprise applications will feature task-specific AI agents by 2026, yet research shows that a mere 6% of organizations have an advanced AI security strategy in place. Only 24% of enterprises have a dedicated AI security governance team.

## Top Priorities for Investment

Cybersecurity is the single greatest barrier to achieving AI strategy goals – 80% of leaders say so, up from 68% in Q1. Data privacy (77%, up from 53% in Q1) and data quality (65%, up from 37% in Q1) have risen sharply as agent-to-agent workflows and tool integrations expand risk.

The analyst consensus emphasizes that governance has shifted from a compliance checkbox to a strategic necessity for scaling agentic AI safely and effectively in 2026.

Based on the search results, here's what recent surveys from McKinsey, Deloitte, and Accenture reveal about agentic AI adoption and enterprise production deployment:

## Current Adoption Status

23% of organizations are scaling agentic AI systems somewhere in their enterprises, and an additional 39% have begun experimenting with AI agents. However, most of those scaling agents are only doing so in one or two functions, and in any given business function, no more than 10% of respondents report scaling AI agents.

## Growth Projections

Deloitte predicts that 25% of enterprises using Gen AI are expected to deploy AI agents in 2025, growing to 50% by 2027. More aggressive projections suggest by the end of 2026, 40% of enterprise applications will include task-specific AI agents.

## The Production Deployment Gap

Despite high investment intentions, there's a significant gap between claimed adoption and actual production deployment. Deloitte surveyed 3,235 leaders between August and September 2025, and agentic AI usage is poised to rise sharply in the next two years, but oversight is lagging, with only one in five companies having a mature model for governance of autonomous AI agents.

According to Futurum's 1H 2025 AI Platforms Decision Maker Survey, 55% of enterprises remain in research or pilot phases for agentic AI, with only 37% reporting advanced GenAI maturity.

## Real-World Deployment Examples

Enterprises are already deploying autonomous AI agents: a financial services company is building agentic workflows to capture meeting actions and track follow-through, an air carrier is using AI agents for customer transactions, and a manufacturer is using AI agents to support new product development.

Based on the search results, here are the key findings from recent CISO surveys regarding AI agent risks and ungoverned agents in enterprise security for 2025-2026:

## Widespread Adoption Outpacing Security

More than 80% of Fortune 500 companies now deploy active AI agents, many built with low-code and no-code tools that put agent creation in the hands of business users, not just developers. 69% of enterprises are already piloting or running early production agent deployments.

## Critical Concerns and Gaps

73% of CISOs are very or critically concerned about AI agent risks, but only 30% have mature safeguards in place to protect them. Zero trust covers human identities but leaves AI agents and NHI largely ungoverned, raising the question: when an AI tool violates a policy or an agent takes a risky action, what enforcement mechanism actually intervenes?

## Visibility Blind Spots

79% of enterprises operate with blindspots, where agents invoke tools, touch data, or trigger actions that the security team cannot fully observe. 32% of organizations identify unsupervised data access by AI agents as a critical threat.

## Real-World Incidents

37% experienced AI agent-caused operational issues in the past twelve months, with 8% significant enough to cause outages or data corruption. 38% point to an agent autonomously moving data to an untrusted location as their top runaway concern, and 24% fear an agent deleting critical configurations or code.

## Weak Enforcement Mechanisms

31% enforce AI security through written policies and employee compliance, another 20% rely on post-event API scanning, endpoint-based controls account for 15%, and inline real-time enforcement for 23%, with the remaining 11% having no AI security policies at all.

Based on my search results, here's a comprehensive overview of AI agent identity, credential management, OAuth, API keys, and secrets sprawl as enterprise risks in 2025-2026:

## Current State of AI Agent Identity Risk

Only 10% of organizations have a well-developed strategy for managing non-human and agentic identities, according to a recent Okta survey, even as AI agents become operational actors across enterprise infrastructure. Fortune 500 data shows AI-agent adoption up 840× year-over-year (from July 2024 to July 2025), with Copilot agent creation growing 1,767%, and at this pace, large enterprises will see agents outnumber employees by the end 2025.

## Credential Sprawl and Secrets Management Issues

Credential sprawl involves hardcoded API keys and static tokens embedded in pipelines and notebooks. AI agents rely on the same kinds of tokens, API keys, and service account credentials that human administrators use for authentication and access to resources, and these secrets often have a long lifespan, significantly increasing their vulnerability to exploitation.

AI agents often receive organization-wide API keys instead of scoped access because fine-grained permission models become operationally complex when agents need to access diverse APIs dynamically, creating persistent credential exposure across the entire AI agent lifecycle.

## Problems with Traditional Authentication

AI agents still rely on static API keys, leaving them exposed to breaches and prompt injection attacks. Since API keys aren't true identities, the solution isn't better secrets management—it's eliminating secrets altogether with workload identity and dynamic credentials that adapt to runtime context.

OAuth's token-based authorization model is primarily designed for static permissions assigned to human users and applications with well-defined scopes. AI agents require more granular and adaptive access control mechanisms, as their permissions may need to change dynamically based on contextual factors such as risk levels, mission objectives, or real-time data analysis.

## Recommended Solutions

Instead of storing long-lived API keys, agents can use OAuth client credentials flows to obtain short-lived access tokens, where client credentials are derived from the workload identity rather than pre-shared secrets, eliminating credential exposure risk entirely.

Dynamic secrets through automated credential lifecycle management with just-in-time provisioning and automatic rotation can eliminate credential sprawl and reduce exposure windows.

A secure token lifecycle includes: Automated rotation of API keys, OAuth tokens, and certificates; Expiration and renewal policies tied to agent roles or behaviors; Scoped, time-bound tokens to limit unnecessary standing privileges; and Revocation workflows triggered by anomalies, deprovisioning events, or risk scores.

## Business Impact of Risks

A single leaked model API key can cost tens of thousands per day in consumed tokens. A compromised connector can silently exfiltrate sensitive data to untrusted LLM endpoints. And as AI systems chain tasks across multiple environments, one compromised agent can trigger cascading privilege escalation.

# Market Research: AI Agent Identity & Governance Products (2025-2026)

## 1. Enterprise Spending Signals

**The signal is strong and backed by real budget data, not just intent surveys.**

- **98% of enterprises** plan to increase AI governance budgets in the next financial year, with an average anticipated **24% budget jump** ([KPMG AI Pulse](https://kpmg.com/us/en/media/news/q4-ai-pulse.html)).
- **Half of executives** plan to allocate **$10-50 million** in the coming year specifically to secure agentic architectures, improve data lineage, and harden model governance ([KPMG AI Pulse](https://kpmg.com/us/en/media/news/q4-ai-pulse.html)).
- **Cybersecurity is now the single greatest barrier** to achieving AI strategy goals: **80% of leaders** say so (up from 68% in Q1 2025), with data privacy concerns jumping from 53% to 77% ([KPMG AI Pulse](https://kpmg.com/us/en/media/news/q4-ai-pulse.html)).
- Gartner projects global cybersecurity spending will reach **$240 billion in 2026**, up 12.5% from $213 billion in 2025 ([Elisity](https://www.elisity.com/blog/2026-cybersecurity-budget-complete-enterprise-planning-guide)).
- **70% of organizations** now dedicate more than 10% of their security budgets to AI-related investments ([Reco.ai CISO Hub](https://www.reco.ai/ciso-hub/what-percentage-security-budget-ai-security)).

**Skeptical note:** The $10-50M figures come from executive surveys about *planned* spending, not actual expenditure. However, the VC funding data (below) confirms capital is actually flowing.

---

## 2. Buyer Pain Points

**CISOs are alarmed and articulate about specific, concrete problems -- this is not theoretical anxiety.**

### The Visibility Gap
- **79% of enterprises** operate with blind spots where agents invoke tools, touch data, or trigger actions that security teams cannot fully observe ([NeuralTrust State of AI Agent Security 2026](https://neuraltrust.ai/guides/the-state-of-ai-agent-security-2026)).
- **Over 3 million AI agents** are now operating within corporations, with only **47.1% actively monitored or secured**, leaving an estimated **1.5 million agents running without oversight** ([CIO.com](https://www.cio.com/article/4083473/shadow-ai-the-hidden-agents-beyond-traditional-governance.html); [Beam.ai](https://beam.ai/agentic-insights/ai-agent-sprawl-new-shadow-it)).

### The Fear/Readiness Gap
- **73% of CISOs** are very or critically concerned about AI agent risks, but only **30%** have mature safeguards in place ([PR Newswire / NeuralTrust](https://www.prnewswire.com/news-releases/the-state-of-ai-agent-security-73-of-cisos-fear-ai-agent-risks-but-only-30-are-ready-302607386.html)).
- Only **6% of organizations** have an advanced AI security strategy; only **24%** have a dedicated AI security governance team ([CSA AI Security Report via Help Net Security](https://www.helpnetsecurity.com/2025/12/24/csa-ai-security-governance-report/)).
- Only **10% of organizations** have a well-developed strategy for managing non-human and agentic identities (Okta survey) ([Aembit](https://aembit.io/blog/ai-agent-identity-security/)).

### Real Incidents, Not Hypotheticals
- **37% of enterprises** experienced AI agent-caused operational issues in the past 12 months; **8%** were severe enough to cause outages or data corruption ([NeuralTrust](https://neuraltrust.ai/guides/the-state-of-ai-agent-security-2026)).
- IBM's 2025 report found shadow AI added an average of **$670,000 to breach costs** where present ([Dunavetsky](https://www.dunavetsky.com/blogs/shadow-ai-governance-crisis.html)).
- **97% of organizations** that suffered AI-related security incidents lacked proper AI access controls ([Dunavetsky](https://www.dunavetsky.com/blogs/shadow-ai-governance-crisis.html)).

### Specific Technical Pain Points
- AI agents often receive **organization-wide API keys** instead of scoped access because fine-grained permission models are operationally complex ([Aembit](https://aembit.io/blog/securing-ai-agents-without-secrets/)).
- Zero trust frameworks cover human identities but **leave AI agents and NHI largely ungoverned** ([Akto](https://www.akto.io/blog/state-of-agentic-ai-security-2025)).
- **44% of organizations** admit business units are deploying AI solutions without IT or security involvement -- classic "shadow AI" ([Help Net Security / Delinea](https://www.helpnetsecurity.com/2025/11/12/delinea-shadow-ai-governance/)).

---

## 3. Market Sizing

### Machine Identity Management Market
- **~$21 billion in 2026**, projected to reach **$60-63 billion by 2035** at a CAGR of 12-13% ([Business Research Insights](https://www.businessresearchinsights.com/market-reports/machine-identity-management-market-102859); [WiseGuy Reports](https://www.wiseguyreports.com/reports/machine-identity-management-market)).
- Non-human identities specifically are growing **40%+ annually**, creating a **$23 billion emerging market segment** for governing AI agents and machine accounts ([Dark Reading](https://www.darkreading.com/identity-access-management-security/vendors-attackers-chase-potential-of-non-human-identities)).

### AI Governance Market
- **$309 million in 2025**, growing to **$419-492 million in 2026** ([Precedence Research](https://www.precedenceresearch.com/ai-governance-market); [Gartner](https://www.gartner.com/en/newsroom/press-releases/2026-02-17-gartner-global-ai-regulations-fuel-billion-dollar-market-for-ai-governance-platforms)).
- Gartner specifically forecasts governance spending to **surpass $1 billion by 2030** ([Gartner](https://www.gartner.com/en/newsroom/press-releases/2026-02-17-gartner-global-ai-regulations-fuel-billion-dollar-market-for-ai-governance-platforms)).
- Broader projections range to **$3.6-4.8 billion by 2033-2034** at a CAGR of 35-36% ([Grand View Research](https://www.grandviewresearch.com/industry-analysis/ai-governance-market-report); [Precedence Research](https://www.precedenceresearch.com/ai-governance-market)).

### Agentic AI Market (Broader)
- The agentic AI market overall is sized at **$9-11 billion in 2026** ([Tech Insider](https://tech-insider.org/agentic-ai-enterprise-2026-market-analysis/)).
- Agentic AI companies raised **$6 billion in equity funding** across 213 rounds in 2025, up 30% from 2024 ([Tracxn](https://tracxn.com/d/sectors/agentic-ai/__oyRAfdUfHPjf2oap110Wis0Qg12Gd8DzULlDXPJzrzs)).

- IAM pulled **$990 million in VC funding**, concentrated in just 6 companies ([Crunchbase](https://news.crunchbase.com/venture/cybersecurity-startup-investment-up-ye-2025/)).

**Skeptical note:** Market sizing estimates for "AI governance" vary wildly depending on where the boundary is drawn. The $309M (2025) figure from Precedence Research is plausible for pure-play governance tooling. The $21B machine identity figure includes traditional certificate/PKI management, not just AI agent identity. The real addressable market for "AI agent identity and governance" specifically is likely in the **$500M-$2B range in 2026**, growing rapidly.

---

## 4. Enterprise Readiness Signals

**Real adoption is happening, but it is shallower than headlines suggest.**

### What the Surveys Actually Say
- **Deloitte's State of AI 2026** (3,235 leaders surveyed, Aug-Sept 2025): **23% of companies** are currently using agentic AI at least moderately. **74%** expect to be doing so within two years ([Deloitte](https://www.deloitte.com/global/en/issues/generative-ai/state-of-ai-in-enterprise.html)).
- However, most companies scaling agents are only doing so in **one or two functions**, and in any given business function, **no more than 10%** report scaling AI agents ([McKinsey](https://www.mckinsey.com/capabilities/quantumblack/our-insights/the-state-of-ai)).
- Gartner predicts **40% of enterprise applications** will feature task-specific AI agents by 2026, up from **less than 5% in 2025** ([Gartner](https://www.gartner.com/en/newsroom/press-releases/2025-08-26-gartner-predicts-40-percent-of-enterprise-apps-will-feature-task-specific-ai-agents-by-2026-up-from-less-than-5-percent-in-2025)).
- **55% of enterprises** remain in research or pilot phases for agentic AI, with only **37%** reporting advanced GenAI maturity (Futurum 1H 2025 survey) ([Klover.ai](https://www.klover.ai/ai-agents-in-enterprise-market-survey-mckinsey-pwc-deloitte-gartner/)).

### The Governance Maturity Deficit
- Only **21%** of companies have a mature governance model for autonomous AI agents ([Deloitte](https://www.deloitte.com/us/en/about/press-room/state-of-ai-report-2026.html)).
- AI strategy preparedness is at ~40%, but **governance readiness trails at 30%**, and **talent readiness is at only 20%** ([Deloitte](https://www.deloitte.com/us/en/about/press-room/state-of-ai-report-2026.html)).

### Shadow AI is Already a Reality
- **75% of workers** are using AI tools at work; **78% of those** are bringing their own AI with no security review ([CIO.com](https://www.cio.com/article/4083473/shadow-ai-the-hidden-agents-beyond-traditional-governance.html)).
- AI-agent adoption by Fortune 500 companies grew **840x year-over-year** (July 2024 to July 2025), with Copilot agent creation growing **1,767%** ([Aembit](https://aembit.io/blog/ai-agent-identity-security/)).
- Enterprises currently use an average of **12 AI agents** and project that figure to **grow 67% within two years** (Salesforce) ([Digital Commerce 360](https://www.digitalcommerce360.com/2026/02/05/salesforce-findings-ai-agent-use-by-2027/)).

**Skeptical note:** The 840x and 1,767% growth figures for agent creation likely include trivial low-code automations and simple chatbot wrappers, not sophisticated autonomous agents. The Deloitte 23% "moderate use" figure is more credible as a measure of meaningful deployment. The real signal is that even shallow adoption is already outpacing governance -- which is exactly the buyer pain point that creates market demand.

## 5. Budget Allocation

**Money is flowing to both incumbents and startups, but startups are winning disproportionate attention for the AI-agent-specific problem.**

### Where the VC Money Is Going
- **Cybersecurity startup investment in 2025: $18 billion**, up 26% from 2024, the highest level in three years ([Crunchbase](https://news.crunchbase.com/venture/cybersecurity-startup-investment-up-ye-2025/)).
- AI security was the **#1 category** for both startup formation and planned enterprise spending ([Help Net Security](https://www.helpnetsecurity.com/2026/02/25/cybersecurity-venture-funding-ai-security-expands/)).

### Incumbent vs. Startup Dynamics
- **IAM pulled $990M in VC**, but concentrated in just **6 companies** -- suggesting winner-take-more dynamics ([Crunchbase](https://news.crunchbase.com/venture/cybersecurity-startup-investment-up-ye-2025/)).
- Gartner notes: enterprises must choose between **established vendors** (financial stability, legacy integration) and **innovative startups** (more targeted solutions, acquisition risk). Market consolidation is expected as buyer requirements clarify ([Gartner](https://www.gartner.com/en/newsroom/press-releases/2026-02-17-gartner-global-ai-regulations-fuel-billion-dollar-market-for-ai-governance-platforms)).
- Incumbent players like **HashiCorp** (dynamic secrets for AI agents), **1Password** (agentic AI authentication), **BeyondTrust** (agent identity governance), and **WorkOS** (agent auth/authz) are all actively positioning products in this space ([HashiCorp](https://developer.hashicorp.com/validated-patterns/vault/ai-agent-identity-with-hashicorp-vault); [1Password](https://1password.com/solutions/agentic-ai); [BeyondTrust](https://www.beyondtrust.com/blog/entry/how-to-govern-ai-agent-identities); [WorkOS](https://workos.com/blog/securing-ai-agents)).
- Recommended enterprise budget framework for AI security: **30-35% to discovery, 25-30% to governance, 25-30% to data protection, 10-15% to threat defense** ([Reco.ai](https://www.reco.ai/ciso-hub/what-percentage-security-budget-ai-security)).

## Summary Assessment: Real Demand vs. Hype

### What is real:
1. **The governance gap is genuine and measurable.** 73% of CISOs are worried, only 30% are ready. 79% have blind spots. 37% have already experienced incidents. These are consistent findings across multiple independent surveys.
2. **Money is actually moving.** $120M for Oasis, $700M for Saviynt, $130M for 7AI -- these are not seed rounds; they are growth-stage bets by Sequoia, Craft, Accel, Index, and Greylock. The capital concentration in NHI/agent identity is a strong demand signal.
3. **The adoption curve creates urgency.** Even conservative estimates show 23% current deployment growing to 74% within two years. Gartner's less-than-5% to 40% prediction for agent-equipped apps is aggressive but directionally supported.
4. **Regulatory tailwinds are building.** Gartner predicts AI regulation will quadruple and extend to 75% of economies by 2030, making governance a compliance necessity, not optional.

### What is hype or uncertain:
1. **Market size figures are unreliable.** Estimates for "AI governance" in 2025 range from $227M to $340M depending on the source. Machine identity at "$21B" includes a lot of traditional PKI that has nothing to do with AI agents.
2. **"40% of enterprise apps will have agents by 2026"** almost certainly includes trivial integrations (autocomplete, simple automation) alongside genuinely autonomous agents. The definition of "agent" is doing a lot of work in these projections.
3. **Shadow AI statistics are likely inflated.** The "3 million AI agents in corporations" figure and "840x growth" numbers likely conflate simple automations with true autonomous agents.
4. **Buy vs. build is unresolved.** Many enterprises may attempt internal solutions before buying, especially for agent identity management that is tightly coupled with their existing IAM infrastructure.

### Net Assessment:
The market demand for AI agent identity and governance products is **real, early-stage, and growing fast**. The specific addressable market for pure-play AI agent identity/governance tooling is likely in the **$500M-$2B range in 2026**, with a path to **$5B+ by 2030** if regulatory pressure and agent adoption continue on current trajectories. The strongest near-term buying signal is from enterprises that are already deploying agents (23% per Deloitte) and discovering they have no governance framework (79% have blind spots). The competitive landscape is fragmented, with both startups and incumbents racing to define the category.
